title: A Support Theorem for Parabolic Stochastic PDEs with Nondegenerate H√∂lder Diffusion Coefficients
overall_summary: The paper proves small-ball probability estimates and a full-support theorem for one-dimensional parabolic stochastic heat equations on [0,1] with Neumann boundary conditions, uniformly bounded drift g, and uniformly elliptic diffusion coefficient ŌÉ that is only H√∂lder continuous in the solution variable. The main lower small-ball bound holds for every probabilistic weak solution when the H√∂lder exponent őĪ is merely positive; a two-sided estimate is proved under őĪ>3/4, matching available strong well-posedness theory. The proof reduces bounded drift and nonzero target paths by Girsanov and path subtraction, compares the nonlinear equation to a Gaussian equation with frozen diffusion coefficient, controls the comparison error using sharp local stochastic-integral estimates reflecting 1:2:4 stochastic heat scaling, and iterates one-slab estimates by the Markov property. For the original invariant-measure problem, the most relevant content is the full-support and small-ball machinery for nondegenerate additive or uniformly elliptic noise, especially as a possible route to topological irreducibility for regularized equations or singular-drift limits if analogous Dirichlet-boundary and singular-drift estimates are available.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the real-valued SPDE on [0,‚ąě) √ó [0,1] with Neumann boundary conditions, ‚ąā_t u(t,x) = (1/2)‚ąā_x^2 u(t,x) + g(t,x,u(t,x)) + ŌÉ(t,x,u(t,x)) dot W(t,x), u(0,x)=u0(x). Let PC_b^2 denote predictable fields whose value, time derivative, and second space derivative are almost surely bounded by a fixed constant. Assume u0 and h are in PC_b^2 and sup_{x‚ąą[0,1]} |u0(x)-h(0,x)| < őĶ/2. Assume that for some D,c_min,c_max>0 and őĪ‚ąą(3/4,1], for all t‚Č•0, x‚ąą[0,1], and r,s‚ąąR one has |ŌÉ(t,x,r)-ŌÉ(t,x,s)| ‚Č§ D|r-s|^őĪ and c_min ‚Č§ |ŌÉ(t,x,r)| ‚Č§ c_max, and also sup_{t,x,r} |g(t,x,r)| < ‚ąě. Then for every ő≤>2‚ąíőĪ there are positive constants A0,A1,A2,A3 and őĶ0, with the dependence specified in the theorem in terms of ő≤,c_min,c_max and sup|g|, such that for every 0<őĶ<őĶ0, A0 exp(‚ąíA1 T / őĶ^(2+4ő≤)) ‚Č§ P(sup_{0‚Č§t‚Č§T, x‚ąą[0,1]} |u(t,x)-h(t,x)| ‚Č§ őĶ) ‚Č§ A2 exp(‚ąíA3 T / ((1+D^2) őĶ^(4+2őĪ))). If instead only őĪ>0 is assumed, then the lower bound still holds. Moreover, there exists D0>0 depending only on c_min and c_max such that, when D<D0, the estimates can be taken with the endpoint value ő≤=2‚ąíőĪ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first reduces to the case g=0 and h=0. For g, write the equation as the driftless equation driven by dot W + ŌÉ^{-1}g and use Girsanov; boundedness of g and the lower ellipticity c_min give an exponential martingale with finite second moment, and Cauchy-Schwarz compares event probabilities under the drifted and driftless laws. For h, set H=‚ąā_t‚ąí(1/2)‚ąā_x^2 and define w(t,x)=u(t,x)‚ąíu0(x)‚ąíh(t,x)+h(0,x); then w has zero initial condition and solves the same type of equation with modified diffusion ŌÉ1(t,x,w)=ŌÉ(t,x,u) and modified drift g1(t,x,w)=g(t,x,u)‚ąíH u0(x)‚ąíHh(t,x)+Hh(0,x), which remains bounded because u0,h‚ąąPC_b^2. After these reductions, the proof uses two quoted Gaussian/small-scale estimates: Proposition 2.1 for stochastic integrals over parabolic boxes and Proposition 2.3 for the Gaussian equation whose ŌÉ does not depend on u. For the upper bound, the proof needs őĪ>3/4 to solve an auxiliary SPDE strongly. It defines the truncation f_őĶ(z)=z for |z|<őĶ and f_őĶ(z)=őĶ z/|z| for |z|>őĶ, solves v with coefficient ŌÉ(t,x,f_őĶ(v)), and observes that v=u until u exits the őĶ-tube. It then compares v with a Gaussian process v_g whose diffusion coefficient is frozen at ŌÉ(t,x,f_őĶ(u0(x))). The difference D=v‚ąív_g is a stochastic convolution with integrand bounded by D(2őĶ)^őĪ by H√∂lder continuity of ŌÉ. On one time slab and at the spatial grid points p_{1j}, the event {|v(p_{1j})|‚Č§őĶ} is contained in {|v_g(p_{1j})|‚Č§2őĶ} ‚ą™ {|D(p_{1j})|‚Č•őĶ}. Hence the one-slab small-ball probability is bounded by a Gaussian grid small-ball probability, controlled by Proposition 2.3, plus a union bound over error probabilities, controlled by Proposition 2.1 and the bound D(2őĶ)^őĪ on the stochastic-integral coefficient. This gives a one-slab upper estimate of order exp(‚ąíc/((1+D^2)őĶ^(2őĪ))). Iterating over about T/őĶ^4 slabs using the Markov property yields the exponent őĶ^(‚ąí4‚ąí2őĪ). For the lower bound, choose a finer time mesh with step c0 őĶ^(4ő≤), where ő≤>1 and őĪ+ő≤>2. Starting from sup_x |u0(x)|‚Č§őĶ/3, freeze the coefficient at u0 and let u_g be the corresponding Gaussian process. Let ŌĄ be the first time sup_x |u(t,x)‚ąíu0(x)|>2őĶ and define a stopped error convolution tilde D using u(s‚ąßŌĄ). If u_g stays within 2őĶ/3 on the slab and ends within őĶ/6, and if sup|tilde D|‚Č§őĶ/6, then u stays in the required őĶ-tube and ends within őĶ/3. Proposition 2.3 gives the Gaussian event probability at least C exp(‚ąíC/őĶ^2), because the refined time slab is shorter than the original parabolic slab. The error term is bounded by covering space with O(őĶ^‚ąí2) intervals and applying Proposition 2.1 with time-scale parameter őĶ^(4ő≤‚ąí4) and coefficient size D(2őĶ)^őĪ; this gives a probability bounded by an exponentially smaller term of order exp(‚ąíc/őĶ^(2(őĪ+ő≤‚ąí1))) up to polynomial factors. Since őĪ+ő≤>2, this is negligible relative to exp(‚ąíC/őĶ^2) for small őĶ, yielding a one-slab lower bound C exp(‚ąíC/őĶ^2). Markov iteration over about T/őĶ^(4ő≤) refined slabs gives the lower exponent őĶ^(‚ąí2‚ąí4ő≤). The final small-D assertion comes from keeping the D-dependence in the error estimate and allowing the borderline őĪ+ő≤=2 when D is sufficiently small.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume őĪ‚ąą(0,1] and the hypotheses of the SPDE setting in Theorem 1.1 that are needed for the lower support estimate. Let őľ be the probability law on C([0,T] √ó [0,1]) of any possible weak solution to the SPDE. Define ő©_{u0}([0,T] √ó [0,1]) = {w‚ąąC([0,T] √ó [0,1]) : w(0,x)=u0(x) for all x‚ąą[0,1]}. Then the topological support of őľ, with C([0,T] √ó [0,1]) endowed with the supremum norm topology, is exactly ő©_{u0}([0,T] √ó [0,1]).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inclusion Supp őľ ‚äÜ ő©_{u0} is immediate from continuity of sample paths and the deterministic initial condition. For the reverse inclusion, Theorem 1.1 gives positive probability for every sufficiently small supremum-norm tube around every admissible smooth bounded target path h with the correct initial value up to the őĶ/2 tolerance. Smooth PC_b^2 paths are dense in the continuous path space with fixed initial condition in the supremum norm, so for any w‚ąąő©_{u0} and any open supremum ball around w one chooses such an h inside a smaller ball and applies the lower bound. Thus every nonempty open neighborhood in ő©_{u0} has positive őľ-mass.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1 drift and target-path reduction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the SPDE of Theorem 1.1 with bounded drift g and uniformly elliptic ŌÉ, one may compare the law with drift g to the driftless law by Girsanov. If P_t is the original law up to time t and Q_t is defined by the exponential martingale with stochastic integral ‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_0^1 ŌÉ^{-1}g(s,x,u(s,x)) W(ds dx) and compensator (1/2)‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_0^1 (ŌÉ^{-1}g(s,x,u(s,x)))^2 dx ds, then dot W + ŌÉ^{-1}g is a space-time white noise under Q_t. For the event A={sup_{s‚ąą[0,T],y‚ąą[0,1]} |u(s,y)‚ąíh(s,y)|<őĶ}, Cauchy-Schwarz gives Q_T(A) ‚Č§ P_T(A)^(1/2) (E_P[(dQ_T/dP_T)^2])^(1/2) ‚Č§ M P_T(A)^(1/2), where M depends only on T, the ellipticity lower bound, and sup|g|. The same comparison with P and Q interchanged, replacing g by ‚ąíg, is used for upper bounds. A separate deterministic shift w(t,x)=u(t,x)‚ąíu0(x)‚ąíh(t,x)+h(0,x) converts estimates around h and u0 into estimates around zero initial data and zero target, at the cost of replacing g by a bounded modified drift involving H=‚ąā_t‚ąí(1/2)‚ąā_x^2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Uniform ellipticity gives |ŌÉ^{-1}g|‚Č§c_min^‚ąí1 sup|g|, so Novikov-type integrability and a finite second moment of the Girsanov density are immediate on finite time intervals. Under the changed measure the shifted noise is white noise, so the equation with drift is represented as a driftless equation under the new law. The displayed Cauchy-Schwarz inequality transfers lower bounds from Q to P and upper bounds from P to Q, up to changing constants. For the path shift, direct substitution into the SPDE gives ‚ąā_t w=(1/2)‚ąā_x^2 w+g1(t,x,w)+ŌÉ1(t,x,w)dot W with g1=g‚ąíHu0‚ąíHh+Hh(0), and boundedness of u0,h and their indicated derivatives ensures g1 is bounded, so the preceding drift reduction applies again.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1, quoted from Athreya-Joseph-Mueller [1]
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G(t,x)=ő£_{n‚ąąZ}(2ŌÄt)^‚ąí1/2 exp(‚ąí(x+n)^2/(2t)) be the heat kernel used in the paper. For a predictable integrand ŌÉ(s,y,u(s,y)) satisfying sup_{s,y}|ŌÉ(s,y,u(s,y))|‚Č§C, define the stochastic convolution N(t,x)=‚ąę_0^t‚ąę_0^1 G(t‚ąís,x‚ąíy) ŌÉ(s,y,u(s,y)) W(dy ds). There exist universal constants K1,K2 such that for all a,őĽ,őĶ>0, P(sup_{0‚Č§t‚Č§a őĶ^4, x‚ąą[0,őĶ^2]} |N(t,x)| > őĽ őĶ) ‚Č§ K1/(1‚ąßsqrt(a)) exp(‚ąíK2 őĽ^2/(C^2 sqrt(a))).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove this estimate; it quotes it from [1, Proposition 3.4 and Remark 3.1]. It is used as a precise small-scale large-deviation estimate for stochastic heat convolutions, encoding the 1:2:4 scaling that on a time interval of length a őĶ^4 and a spatial interval of length őĶ^2 the stochastic convolution has typical size őĶ. In the present paper the estimate is applied to comparison-error convolutions whose integrand is bounded by a H√∂lder coefficient difference, for example C=D(2őĶ)^őĪ, and then combined with spatial union bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The estimate in Proposition 2.1 remains valid, with the same form of constants, if the spatial supremum over x‚ąą[0,őĶ^2] is replaced by x‚ąą[(k‚ąí1)őĶ^2,kőĶ^2] for any positive integer k satisfying kőĶ^2‚Č§1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper explains that the proof in [1] only uses a modulus-of-continuity estimate for the stochastic convolution that is independent of the spatial location of the interval, together with a path of grid points from the initial corner to the point (t,x). For a shifted interval one starts the grid path at (0,(k‚ąí1)őĶ^2) instead of (0,0), so the same bound applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3, quoted from Athreya-Joseph-Mueller [1]
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Use the parabolic mesh t_i=i c0 őĶ^4 and x_j=j c1 őĶ^2, with c0 small and c1^2=őł c0 as fixed in Section 2.2, and write I_n=[t_n,t_{n+1}], p_{ij}=(t_i,x_j), n1=min{n‚Č•1:t_n>T}, and n2=min{n‚Č•1:x_n>1}. Define A_n={|u(t_{n+1},x)|‚Č§őĶ/3 for all x‚ąą[0,1], and |u(t,x)|‚Č§őĶ for all t‚ąąI_n and x‚ąą[0,1]} and F_n={|u(p_{nj})|<őĶ for all j‚Č§n2‚ąí2}. Under the assumptions of Theorem 1.1, with additionally g=0, u0‚Č°0, and ŌÉ(t,x,u) independent of u, there are constants őĶ0,C4,C5>0 depending only on the ellipticity bounds such that for 0<őĶ<őĶ0, P(F_n | ‚ą©_{k=0}^{n‚ąí1} F_k) ‚Č§ C4 exp(‚ąíC5 őĶ^‚ąí2). Also there are constants C6,C7>0 depending only on the ellipticity bounds such that for 0<őĶ<őĶ0, P(A_n | ‚ą©_{k=0}^{n‚ąí1} A_k) ‚Č• C6 exp(‚ąíC7 őĶ^‚ąí2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This proposition is quoted from [1, Proposition 2.1] and is not reproved in the paper. It supplies the Gaussian one-slab input for both sides of Theorem 1.1. The upper estimate says that, conditionally on previous grid-time smallness, the probability of being small at all grid points on the next time layer is at most exponentially small with exponent őĶ^‚ąí2. The lower estimate says that, conditionally on previous slab smallness, the Gaussian equation has at least exponentially small probability exp(‚ąíCőĶ^‚ąí2) to remain in an őĶ-tube on the next slab and end in an őĶ/3-tube. Han applies these estimates to frozen-coefficient Gaussian comparison processes such as u_g and v_g.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Derived one-slab nonlinear comparison estimates in Sections 2.3 and 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the reduced setting g=0 and h=0, the proof derives the following one-slab estimates. For the upper bound, with the parabolic time mesh I_{n‚ąí1}=[t_{n‚ąí1},t_n] of length c0 őĶ^4 and B_n={|u(t,x)|‚Č§őĶ for all t‚ąąI_{n‚ąí1}, x‚ąą[0,1]}, the paper obtains for small őĶ an estimate of the form P(B_n | ‚ą©_{j=1}^{n‚ąí1} B_j) ‚Č§ exp(‚ąíc/((1+D^2) őĶ^(2őĪ))), after constants are adjusted. For the lower bound, with the refined time mesh hat t_n=n c0 őĶ^(4ő≤), hat I_n=[hat t_n,hat t_{n+1}], and hat A_n={|u(hat t_{n+1},x)|‚Č§őĶ/3 for all x‚ąą[0,1], and |u(t,x)|‚Č§őĶ for all t‚ąąhat I_n, x‚ąą[0,1]}, if ő≤>1 and őĪ+ő≤>2 then for small őĶ one has P(hat A_n | ‚ą©_{j=0}^{n‚ąí1} hat A_j) ‚Č• C exp(‚ąíC‚Ä≤/őĶ^2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the upper estimate, the truncated process v with coefficient ŌÉ(t,x,f_őĶ(v)) agrees with u as long as u remains in the őĶ-tube. Freezing the coefficient at the initial profile gives a Gaussian process v_g. At each spatial grid point, smallness of v implies either smallness of v_g at twice the radius or largeness of the difference D=v‚ąív_g. Proposition 2.3 controls the first alternative over all grid points, while Proposition 2.1 and the H√∂lder bound |ŌÉ(f_őĶ(v))‚ąíŌÉ(f_őĶ(u0))|‚Č§D(2őĶ)^őĪ control the second after a union bound. For the lower estimate, the process is compared with a Gaussian process u_g with coefficient frozen at u0, and the error is stopped at the first time u moves more than 2őĶ from u0. If the Gaussian process stays small and the stopped error is at most őĶ/6, then u satisfies hat A_0. Proposition 2.3 gives the Gaussian probability at least exp(‚ąíC/őĶ^2). Proposition 2.1 on the refined time scale, together with spatial covering and the bound D(2őĶ)^őĪ on the stopped coefficient difference, makes the error probability exponentially smaller when őĪ+ő≤>2. The Markov property then makes these estimates conditional for every slab.

proof_techniques.name: Girsanov removal of bounded drift under uniform ellipticity
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the fact that when |ŌÉ| is bounded below and g is uniformly bounded, the drift term can be absorbed into a shift of the white noise: g+ŌÉ dot W = ŌÉ(dot W+ŌÉ^‚ąí1 g). The exponential martingale for ŌÉ^‚ąí1 g has controlled moments, so probabilities of path events under the drifted and driftless equations are comparable via Cauchy-Schwarz, with constants depending on finite time, the ellipticity lower bound, and sup|g|.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original additive-noise equation this is directly relevant to smooth bounded drift approximations of the singular drift, although the bounds for G_{1/n} are not uniform in n. A singular őī0 drift would require an additional argument replacing bounded Girsanov control by an integrability or limiting/local-time estimate.

proof_techniques.name: Subtract a smooth target path to reduce support around h to support around zero
proof_techniques.description: Given a smooth enough predictable target h and initial profile u0, the paper defines w=u‚ąíu0‚ąíh+h(0). The transformed process has zero initial condition and target zero, while the price is a modified drift involving Hh and Hu0, where H=‚ąā_t‚ąí(1/2)‚ąā_x^2. Because h and u0 are PC_b^2, the modified drift remains bounded and is then removed by the Girsanov step.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an analogous support theorem is sought for the Dirichlet problem, this suggests choosing smooth bridges h between an initial profile and a target endpoint, respecting the boundary, and reducing the question to a small-ball estimate around zero.

proof_techniques.name: Freeze the diffusion coefficient and compare to a Gaussian stochastic heat equation
proof_techniques.description: The nonlinear equation is compared on one time slab with a Gaussian equation whose diffusion coefficient is frozen at the initial profile and therefore no longer depends on the evolving solution. The difference is a stochastic convolution whose integrand is the coefficient difference. H√∂lder continuity of ŌÉ gives a deterministic bound on this integrand as long as the solution is localized in an őĶ-tube, enabling Gaussian small-ball estimates for the frozen process plus stochastic-convolution tail estimates for the error.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the original additive-noise problem ŌÉ is constant, so this comparison error would vanish; the remaining difficulty lies in the singular drift rather than multiplicative noise. This makes the paper's Gaussian small-ball and path-support ideas especially plausible for additive-noise regularizations.

proof_techniques.name: Use sharp local 1:2:4 stochastic heat scaling estimates
proof_techniques.description: The key stochastic-integral estimate controls the supremum of a stochastic heat convolution on boxes with time length a őĶ^4 and spatial length őĶ^2, showing Gaussian tails at amplitude őĶ. The paper combines this with spatial covering to control the supremum over the whole interval [0,1]. This is the probabilistic substitute for a deterministic Stroock-Varadhan control problem, which may be ill-posed when coefficients are merely H√∂lder.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For Dirichlet boundary conditions, an analogous local estimate for the Dirichlet heat kernel would provide the basic small-ball ingredient needed to prove transition probabilities charge neighborhoods of arbitrary continuous paths.

proof_techniques.name: Refine the time mesh to handle H√∂lder coefficient errors
proof_techniques.description: When ŌÉ is only őĪ-H√∂lder, the coefficient-freezing error is of size őĶ^őĪ rather than őĶ. To make this error negligible in the lower bound, the paper uses a shorter time step c0 őĶ^(4ő≤) instead of the natural c0 őĶ^4. In Proposition 2.1 this introduces the parameter őĶ^(4ő≤‚ąí4), producing an error exponent őĶ^(‚ąí2(őĪ+ő≤‚ąí1)); choosing őĪ+ő≤>2 makes this exponent stronger than the Gaussian small-ball exponent őĶ^‚ąí2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original equation has additive noise and does not need this H√∂lder-diffusion refinement, the general idea of changing the time scale to dominate approximation errors may be useful when passing from regularized drifts G_{1/n} to the őī0 drift.

proof_techniques.name: Markov iteration of one-slab small-ball estimates
proof_techniques.description: After proving uniform one-slab estimates conditional on the past, the paper multiplies them over many slabs using the Markov property. A one-slab lower bound of order exp(‚ąíCőĶ^‚ąí2) over about T/őĶ^(4ő≤) refined slabs yields exp(‚ąíCT/őĶ^(2+4ő≤)); a one-slab upper bound with exponent őĶ^(‚ąí2őĪ) over about T/őĶ^4 slabs yields exp(‚ąíCT/őĶ^(4+2őĪ)).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For invariant-measure questions, this type of estimate can be used to establish finite-time irreducibility: choose a path from the starting state to a desired neighborhood and show the transition kernel assigns it positive probability.

proof_techniques.name: Path-space full support implies endpoint support
proof_techniques.description: The corollary turns positive probability of small tubes around smooth paths into full support of the entire law on C([0,T]√ó[0,1]). Once one has path-space full support, endpoint support follows by selecting continuous paths with prescribed terminal profile and considering the endpoint map.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the same path-space support statement can be proved for the SPDE with őī0 drift and Dirichlet boundary, then the Markov semigroup would be topologically irreducible on C_0([0,1]), a standard ingredient in uniqueness of invariant measures when combined with a suitable strong Feller, asymptotic strong Feller, or coupling property.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes that for additive noise ŌÉ=Id and drift g that is not too singular, one can often remove the drift by Girsanov and obtain full support from the full support of white noise; it cites Ling-Riedel-Scheutzow [12] for a related finite-dimensional SDE example with singular drift. Remark 1.3 says the őĪ>3/4 assumption is tied to available strong well-posedness results for H√∂lder ŌÉ; the lower bound itself only needs őĪ>0, while the upper-bound proof requires solving an auxiliary SPDE with the same H√∂lder regularity on the same probability space. Remark 1.4 says the arguments extend from [0,1] to finite intervals [0,J], and at least the lower bound extends to vector-valued solutions; the paper itself uses Neumann boundary conditions and does not prove a Dirichlet-boundary version. The main external input for Gaussian small-ball estimates is Athreya-Joseph-Mueller [1], Small ball probabilities and a support theorem for the stochastic heat equation, Annals of Probability 2021, which the paper uses as a black box through Proposition 2.1 and Proposition 2.3.
why_relevant: This paper extends support theorems to rougher coefficients and bounded drifts. Its full-support estimates are relevant because the target equation is obtained as a singular drift limit and one may need robust support/irreducibility arguments.
